anemia.name: Anemia
anemia.description: Low red blood cell count or hemoglobin levels
diet: Increase iron-rich foods: spinach, lentils, red meat, beans, fortified cereals
Add Vitamin C sources with meals: oranges, tomatoes, bell peppers (improves iron absorption)
Include Vitamin B12 sources: eggs, dairy, fish, fortified foods
Add folate-rich foods: leafy greens, legumes, asparagus
Consider iron-fortified bread and cereals
lifestyle: Avoid tea or coffee with meals (tannins block iron absorption)
Cook in cast iron pots (adds dietary iron)
Space calcium supplements away from iron-rich meals
Get adequate sleep (7-8 hours) for blood cell production
Moderate exercise to stimulate red blood cell production
followup: Retest CBC (Complete Blood Count) in 4-6 weeks
Consider iron studies (serum ferritin, TIBC) if anemia persists
Consult doctor if symptoms like fatigue, weakness, or dizziness occur
Check for underlying causes if anemia doesn't improve with diet
warnings: Do not take iron supplements without medical advice
Excessive iron can be harmful - follow prescribed dosage only

iron_deficiency.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
iron_deficiency.description: Anemia caused by insufficient iron levels
diet: Prioritize heme iron sources: red meat, organ meats, shellfish (better absorbed)
Non-heme iron sources: beans, lentils, tofu, spinach, fortified cereals
Pair iron-rich foods with Vitamin C for better absorption
Avoid calcium-rich foods during iron-rich meals
Include dried fruits: raisins, apricots, prunes
lifestyle: Avoid antacids around mealtimes (reduce iron absorption)
Limit coffee and tea consumption, especially with meals
Consider cooking in cast iron cookware
Address any sources of blood loss (heavy menstruation, GI issues)
followup: Get iron studies: serum iron, ferritin, TIBC, transferrin saturation
Retest CBC in 4-6 weeks after dietary changes
If female with heavy periods, discuss with gynecologist
Rule out GI blood loss if iron deficiency persists
warnings: Iron supplements can cause constipation and stomach upset
Take iron supplements as prescribed, not on empty stomach

kidney_concern.name: Kidney Function Concern
diet: Stay well hydrated: 8-10 glasses of water daily
Reduce sodium/salt intake: avoid processed foods, pickles, papad
Moderate protein intake: don't exceed daily requirements
Limit high-purine foods if uric acid elevated: organ meats, shellfish, beer
Increase fruits and vegetables for antioxidants
Reduce phosphorus if advised: limit dairy, nuts, seeds
lifestyle: Monitor blood pressure regularly (high BP damages kidneys)
Control blood sugar if diabetic
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin) without medical advice
Quit smoking (damages blood vessels including kidney vessels)
Maintain healthy weight
Exercise regularly but avoid excessive protein supplements
followup: Retest kidney function (KFT) in 2-4 weeks
Get urine routine and microscopy test
Check for protein in urine (microalbuminuria)
Monitor blood pressure
Consult nephrologist if values remain elevated
warnings: Certain herbal supplements can harm kidneys - consult doctor first
Report any changes in urination pattern to doctor

infection.name: Possible Infection/Inflammation
infection.description: Elevated white blood cell count suggesting infection
diet: Stay hydrated: water, clear soups, herbal teas
Vitamin C rich foods: citrus fruits, berries, bell peppers
Zinc sources: nuts, seeds, legumes, whole grains
Probiotic foods: yogurt, kefir (support immune system)
Anti-inflammatory foods: turmeric, ginger, garlic
Avoid sugar and processed foods (suppress immune function)
lifestyle: Get adequate rest and sleep
Practice good hygiene: frequent handwashing
Avoid contact with sick individuals
Manage stress (chronic stress weakens immunity)
Stay home if you have fever or infection symptoms
followup: If symptomatic (fever, pain), consult doctor immediately
Retest CBC in 1-2 weeks if asymptomatic
Get CRP or ESR if inflammation suspected
Complete any prescribed antibiotic course fully
warnings: Complete full course of antibiotics if prescribed
Seek immediate care for high fever (>103°F) or severe symptoms

leukopenia.name: Low WBC (Leukopenia)
leukopenia.description: Low white blood cell count affecting immunity
diet: Protein-rich foods: lean meats, fish, eggs, legumes
Vitamin B12 and folate: leafy greens, fortified cereals, eggs
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, chickpeas, cashews
Copper sources: shellfish, nuts, seeds, whole grains
Avoid raw or undercooked foods (infection risk)
lifestyle: Practice strict hygiene to prevent infections
Avoid crowded places during flu season
Wear mask in high-risk environments
Avoid contact with sick individuals
Get adequate sleep for immune recovery
Avoid smoking and alcohol
followup: Retest CBC in 2-4 weeks
Check Vitamin B12 and folate levels
Consider bone marrow evaluation if WBC remains low
Review medications that may cause low WBC
Consult hematologist if persistent
warnings: Report any fever immediately to doctor
Low WBC increases infection risk - take precautions
Some medications can cause low WBC - review with doctor

low_rbc_only.name: Low RBC Count
low_rbc_only.description: Isolated low red blood cell count
diet: Iron-rich foods: red meat, spinach, lentils, beans
Vitamin B12 sources: eggs, dairy, fish, fortified cereals
Folate sources: leafy greens, legumes, citrus fruits
Vitamin C to enhance iron absorption
Adequate protein for blood cell production
lifestyle: Ensure adequate sleep for blood cell production
Moderate regular exercise
Stay hydrated
Avoid excessive alcohol consumption
followup: Retest CBC in 4-6 weeks
Check reticulocyte count to assess bone marrow response
Consider iron studies and vitamin levels
Rule out chronic blood loss if RBC remains low
warnings: Monitor for symptoms: fatigue, weakness, shortness of breath

normal.name: Normal Results
diet: Maintain balanced diet with all food groups
Include fruits and vegetables daily
Stay hydrated with adequate water intake
Limit processed foods and excess sugar
Include lean proteins and whole grains
lifestyle: Continue regular physical activity (150 min/week)
Maintain healthy sleep schedule (7-8 hours)
Manage stress through relaxation techniques
Avoid smoking and limit alcohol
Regular health checkups annually
followup: Annual health checkup recommended
Continue monitoring if any chronic conditions
Update vaccinations as recommended

general_advice.sleep: Aim for 7-8 hours of quality sleep
general_advice.exercise: At least 150 minutes of moderate exercise per week
general_advice.stress: Practice stress management: meditation, deep breathing, hobbies
general_advice.checkups: Regular annual health checkups are important

child.notes: Children's reference ranges differ from adults. Consult pediatrician for interpretation.
child.diet_modifier: Focus on growth-supporting nutrients. Ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D.

teenager.notes: Teenage years have fluctuating values. Iron needs increase, especially for menstruating females.
teenager.diet_modifier: Increased iron needs. Avoid junk food. Ensure calcium for bone development.

adult.notes: Standard adult ranges apply. Focus on prevention and maintenance.
adult.diet_modifier: Balanced diet. Limit processed foods. Maintain healthy weight.

senior.notes: Some values naturally change with age. More frequent monitoring recommended.
senior.diet_modifier: May need B12 supplementation. Ensure adequate protein. Watch kidney function.

pregnant.notes: Pregnancy alters many blood values. Anemia is common. Close monitoring needed.
pregnant.diet_modifier: Increased iron and folate needs. Prenatal vitamins essential.

male.notes: Higher hemoglobin and RBC ranges. Watch for polycythemia if values too high.

female.notes: Lower hemoglobin ranges. Iron deficiency more common due to menstruation. Monitor during pregnancy.
